title: Evangelion as Satire
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion as Satire             Quote Post by GAP » Wed Aug 08, 2012 8:33 pm Could Eva work as a satire of super robots where they play traditional tropes in parodic, satrical purposes?

[]: Re: Evangelion as Satire             Quote Post by PolarisX » Wed Aug 08, 2012 9:36 pm GAP wrote: Could Eva work as a satire of super robots where they play traditional tropes in parodic, satrical purposes?

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Wed Aug 08, 2012 10:55 pm There were certainly some moments in the Magmadiver episode (such as the concept of 'therrmal expansion' being explained early in the episode only for it to turn out later on to be the key to defeating Sandalphon) that seemed to reference established tropes from the seventies/eighties Super Robot genre.

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Thu Aug 23, 2012 2:11 pm There are quite a few jabs at shows like Mazinger and Super Robot Red Baron ...the concept of the EVAs going "Berzerker" comes, I think, from "Red Baron"--it can only go for about one minute in full-power mode before exploding. "Mazinger" had the premise of the young man's father going off to build the giant robot that can save humanity; EVA plays that out realistically.

[]:             Quote Post by EvaBrothers » Thu Aug 23, 2012 2:57 pm My first approach to NGE was that of a 'Giant Super Robot' fan. Then it ended up being a different thing, but the first two episodes, for example, show a similar plot to many 'Giant Robot' series: the hero lives a (kind of) normal life but has something special (a power, a personality trait, someone in the family tree, etc.) that gets him to become the 'chosen one' to pilot the Giant Robot-- usually a robot he doesn't know how to pilot, is given instructions and miraculously manages to defeat the enemy. :)

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Thu Aug 23, 2012 3:41 pm There also the subject of the actual fights againstt he monsters themselves, the fights are being portrayed as being rather brutal as opposed to the wuxia-esques that we nomrally see in super robots.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Thu Aug 23, 2012 4:32 pm This probably belongs in discussion, but yeah, it could definitely work as satire. It does a good enough job of subverting certain mecha tropes, I guess it's just up to the viewer, or even Anno, to decide whether or not it was done with satirical intent. Satire's a pretty broad definition so you could really pigeonhole a lot into it.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri Aug 31, 2012 2:38 pm I mean I guess you could say it's satire in the same way that something like Watchmen is satire. Which I don't think technically falls under the definition of satire, but certainly comes close (heavy reliance on irony, a targeted critique of something, etc.).

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Tue Sep 04, 2012 8:18 pm GAP wrote: There also the subject of the actual fights againstt he monsters themselves, the fights are being portrayed as being rather brutal as opposed to the wuxia-esques that we nomrally see in super robots.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Sep 04, 2012 8:37 pm The Evas have an explicit lack of built in weaponry except for useless knives and those spiky thingamabobs that Asuka has one time and apparently have an incredibly specific and not too helpful use. This is even though it would make a great deal of sense just to bolt the rifles to their arms rather than transport them into combat separately and force the pilots to take a risk to pick them up. The most satirey battle is probably the initial confrontation with Ramiel, in which the Invincible Super Robot gets its ass kicked in like two seconds.

[]:             Quote Post by Hyper Shinchan » Tue Sep 04, 2012 11:17 pm Chuckman wrote: The Evas have an explicit lack of built in weaponry except for useless knives and those spiky thingamabobs that Asuka has one time and apparently have an incredibly specific and not too helpful use.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Sep 05, 2012 9:00 am Hyper Shinchan wrote: That's because they play around with the concept of "real robot" too to some extent, the lack of extensive fixed weaponry is an almost constant feature of real robots, in theory it should gives greater flexibility of use, although it's hardly ever the case in Evangelion. Anyway Jet Alone always looked to me like a satire/parody of "anime mecha".

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Wed Sep 05, 2012 1:37 pm If Rebuild counts for anything, stumpy partial Evas don't work too well. They're hard to sync with and don't seem to perform all that hot in combat. Mari certainly had things easier on a dry run in a foreign Eva than her own, defective unit. But that assumes Rebuild counts for anything.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 05, 2012 3:39 pm Chuckman wrote: The Evas have an explicit lack of built in weaponry except for useless knives

[]:             Quote Post by Murmur » Wed Sep 05, 2012 3:44 pm No it wouldn't. If you want a humanoid design (you so very don't, but if you're stuck with one you are) you want that sort of versatility (particularly given that AT Fields are in the mix, meaning ranged weapons often won't be of much use). If the pilots can't reliably neutralize ATFs at range those weapons are dead weight. Better to strip 'em down and give the Eva a basic weapon (i.e. the knife) and add others as needed. And whaddya know, that basic weapon proved more useful than any other in the show!

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 05, 2012 3:47 pm Murmur wrote: I'm not quite sure that giving fingers to a robot is the best solution.

[]:             Quote Post by Murmur » Wed Sep 05, 2012 3:50 pm Bagheera wrote: It's not. It's absolutely stupid, but that's not the point. The point is that, if you're stuck with a humanoid design, it makes sense to play to its strengths rather than bolting it down with hardware it won't regularly use.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Sep 05, 2012 3:55 pm Bagheera wrote: Took out an MPE

[]:             Quote Post by Der Kommissar » Wed Sep 05, 2012 4:41 pm As Reichu said, Tunniel was nothing impressive compared to Zeruel. That they'd managed to contain it, dissect it, and apparently begin to attempt to hijack it tells volumes about how unimpressive of an Angel it was. And we do have Mari actually bitching out loud about synch problems in her unit. Anyway, Evas aren't machines to begin with. They're monsterous, hideously mutated what-humanity-could-have-beens. Their biological abilities to ignore the fuck out of what we know of physics as well as to project an AT field means you're stuck with the form it comes in (humanoid) whether you like it or not. *edit* And how appropriate, I am now tunniel-ranked.

[]:             Quote Post by Na7e » Wed Sep 05, 2012 4:47 pm Der Kommissar wrote: As Reichu said, Tunniel was nothing impressive compared to Zeruel. That they'd managed to contain it, dissect it, and apparently begin to attempt to hijack it tells volumes about how unimpressive of an Angel it was.
